licenseChecklist: All visuals should be locally generated or rendered as in-app SVG diagrams.
Review generated visuals for classroom accuracy before export.
Do not keep rasterized labels, formulas, or chart text in the final deck.
presentationWarnings: Do not include untaught future-session content (e.g., Mole concept details).
Do not accept cluttered or placeholder-only visual panels in the final deck.

learningOutcomesCovered: Define acids and bases based on sensory properties.
List natural sources of common acids and bases.
Identify the color changes of litmus, turmeric, and phenolphthalein in acidic/basic solutions.
Explain why indicators are used instead of tasting chemicals.

slides.presentonMarkdown:
# The World of Acids and Bases: Taste, Touch, and Indicators
> The World of Acids and Bases: Taste, Touch, and Indicators
- Acids, Bases and Salts
- Science (086) | Class X / 10
- 45 minute session

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: title_identity -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Introduce the session and set the learning purpose.
Purpose: Open the lesson, set expectations, and establish relevance.
Explain: This session introduces Class X students to the fundamental properties of acids and bases. Students will explore historical context (Lavoisier), sensory characteristics (sour vs. bitter/soapy), natural occurr...
Explain: To establish the foundational vocabulary of acids and bases.
Explain: To connect abstract chemical concepts to tangible daily experiences.
Explain: To introduce the scientific method of using indicators for identification.
Ask: Why do we not taste acids and bases in a laboratory?
-->

slides.presentonMarkdown:
# What We Will Learn
> Define acids and bases based on sensory properties.
- List natural sources of common acids and bases.
- Identify the color changes of litmus, turmeric, and phenolphthalein in acidic/basic solutions.
- Explain why indicators are used instead of tasting chemicals.

<!-- purpose: learning_outcomes -->

Explain: List natural sources of common acids and bases.
Explain: Identify the color changes of litmus, turmeric, and phenolphthalein in acidic/basic solutions.
Explain: Explain why indicators are used instead of tasting chemicals.
Explain: Students will be able to define acids and bases based on sensory characteristics.
-->

> Basic understanding of matter and its states
- Familiarity with common household substances (lemon juice, soap, vinegar)
- Concept that different substances have different properties

Ask: If a solution turns red litmus blue, what is its nature?
Listen for: Because they can be corrosive or harmful to the body.
Listen for: It is basic.
Transition: Use the recall responses to bridge into the lesson hook.
-->

slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Topic Introduction
> Acids and bases are two fundamental classes of chemicals with opposite properties.
- Acids and bases are two fundamental classes of chemicals with opposite properties.

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: topic_introduction -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Introduce the central idea and vocabulary.
Purpose: Define the idea clearly before moving into deeper explanation.
Explain: Acids and bases are two fundamental classes of chemicals with opposite properties. Acids taste sour and turn blue litmus red, while bases taste bitter/soapy and turn red litmus blue. We use indicators like tu...
Correct: Students may think all sour things are acids and vice versa without understanding chemical definitions.
Board work: Acids = Sour, Blue Litmus -> Red
Board work: Bases = Soapy/Bitter, Red Litmus -> Blue
Transition: Move from the definition into the first core concept.
-->
learningOutcomeIds: Define acids and bases based on sensory properties.
List natural sources of common acids and bases.
slides.teacherIntent: Introduce the central idea and vocabulary.
slides.studentTakeaway: Understand the main concept and basic terms.
bulletPoints: Acids and bases are two fundamental classes of chemicals with opposite properties.
onSlideText: Acids and bases are two fundamental classes of chemicals with opposite properties.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Introduce the central idea and vocabulary.
Purpose: Define the idea clearly before moving into deeper explanation.

Explain: Acids and bases are two fundamental classes of chemicals with opposite properties. Acids taste sour and turn blue litmus red, while bases taste bitter/soapy and turn red litmus blue. We use indicators like tu...
Correct: Students may think all sour things are acids and vice versa without understanding chemical definitions.
Board work: Acids = Sour, Blue Litmus -> Red
Board work: Bases = Soapy/Bitter, Red Litmus -> Blue
Transition: Move from the definition into the first core concept.

slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Acids
> Substances that taste sour and turn blue litmus red.
- Sour
- Hydrogen ions (H+)
- Acidus
- They react with metals and carbonates but p...

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: core_concept_a -->

Purpose: Build the first core concept clearly.
Purpose: Teach the first core idea with direct explanation and examples.
Explain: Acids are characterized by their ability to release hydrogen ions (H+) in water, though at this stage we focus on sensory properties. They react with metals and carbonates but primarily identified here by the...
Explain: Lemon juice, Tamarind, Curd
Explain: Formic acid in ant stings
Explain: We classify substances based on their properties. Two major groups in chemistry are Acids and Bases.
Explain: The word 'Acid' comes from the Latin 'acidus', meaning sour (like Lavoisier named them).
-->

slides.teachingBeat: Explain the second concept with visual emphasis.
mustTeach: Substances that taste bitter, feel soapy to touch, and turn red litmus blue.
Bases often release hydroxide ions (OH-) in water. They are the opposite of acids in terms of sensory properties but share reactivity with indicators.
Soap solution, Baking soda (Sodium bicarbonate)
Lime water (Calcium hydroxide)
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Bases (Alkalis)
> Bitter
> Soapy
- Alkali
- Substances that taste bitter, feel soapy to touch, and turn red litmus blue.
- Bases often release hydroxide ions (OH-) in water.
![Bases (Alkalis)](http://host.docker.internal:3002/api/ppt-assets/f3bd372defb48c0f3f2661265a4ba9d283021947.svg)

<!-- purpose: core_concept_b_visual -->

Explain: Soap solution, Baking soda (Sodium bicarbonate)
Explain: Lime water (Calcium hydroxide)
Explain: Bitter
Explain: Soapy

slides.teachingBeat: Model the thinking process step by step.
mustTeach: Testing vinegar with blue litmus paper.
Adding phenolphthalein to a glass of water vs. soapy water.
Turmeric stain on a white shirt turning red after washing with soap.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Worked Example
> Testing vinegar with blue litmus paper.
> Adding phenolphthalein to a glass of water vs. soapy water.
- Turmeric stain on a white shirt turning red after washing with soap.
![Worked Example](http://host.docker.internal:3002/api/ppt-assets/a7d347b018842eb328f367f41e6eb07b2caa7f02.svg)

<!-- layout: board-example -->
<!-- purpose: worked_example -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Model the thinking process step by step.
Purpose: Model the process out loud and show how to think through the example.
Explain: Ask students to predict whether lemon juice or baking soda solution will turn red litmus blue, then verify.
Explain: Have students identify the color change when turmeric is added to soap water vs. vinegar.
Ask: If I add phenolphthalein to a solution and it turns pink, is it acidic or basic?
Listen for: It is Basic.
Board work: Draw a table with columns: Indicator, Acidic Effect, Basic Effect.
-->

mustTeach: The Color-Changing Detective
Teacher prepares three small beakers: one with vinegar (acid), one with soap water (base), and one with plain water (neutral).
Distribute blue litmus paper, red litmus paper, turmeric paste on a slide, and phenolphthalein solution to groups.
Students dip the papers into each beaker or add drops of indicator to observe color changes.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Guided Practice
> The Color-Changing Detective
- Teacher prepares three small beakers: one with vinegar (acid), one with soap water (base), and one with plain water (...
- Distribute blue litmus paper, red litmus paper, turmeric paste on a slide, and phenolphthalein solution to groups.
- Students dip the papers into each beaker or add drops of indicator to observe color changes.

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: guided_practice -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Help students practice with support.
Purpose: Coach students through a supported practice task.
Explain: Teacher prepares three small beakers: one with vinegar (acid), one with soap water (base), and one with plain water (neutral).
Explain: Distribute blue litmus paper, red litmus paper, turmeric paste on a slide, and phenolphthalein solution to groups.
Explain: Students dip the papers into each beaker or add drops of indicator to observe color changes.
Explain: Record observations in a table: Substance | Indicator Used | Color Change | Nature (Acid/Base/Neutral).
Explain: Discuss why turmeric is useful for detecting soap but not vinegar.
-->

mustTeach: We learned that acids are sour (e.g., lemon) and bases are soapy/bitter. We use indicators like litmus paper to identify them safely without tasting.
Turmeric turns red in base, Phenolphthalein turns pink in base.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Key Takeaways
> We learned that acids are sour (e.g., lemon) and bases are soapy/bitter.
- We learned that acids are sour (e.g., lemon) and bases are soapy/bitter.
- Turmeric turns red in base, Phenolphthalein turns pink in base.

<!-- purpose: summary -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Summarize the important points clearly.
Purpose: Synthesize the lesson and make the memory anchors explicit.
Explain: We learned that acids are sour (e.g., lemon) and bases are soapy/bitter. We use indicators like litmus paper to identify them safely without tasting.
Explain: Turmeric turns red in base, Phenolphthalein turns pink in base.
Explain: Acids = Sour, Blue Litmus -> Red
Explain: Bases = Soapy/Bitter, Red Litmus -> Blue
Explain: Indicators: Turmeric (Yellow->Red in Base), Phenolphthalein (Colorless->Pink in Base)
-->

pdf.documentTitle: The World of Acids and Bases: Taste, Touch, and Indicators - Student Revision Notes
keyInformation: We learned that acids are sour (e.g., lemon) and bases are soapy/bitter. We use indicators like litmus paper to identify them safely without tasting.
Turmeric turns red in base, Phenolphthalein turns pink in base.

docx.outlineTitle: The World of Acids and Bases: Taste, Touch, and Indicators - Teacher Lesson Outline
sections: This session introduces Class X students to the fundamental properties of acids and bases. Students will explore historical context (Lavoisier), sensory characteristics (sour vs. bitter/soapy), natural occurrences in...

Acids and bases are two fundamental classes of chemicals with opposite properties. Acids taste sour and turn blue litmus red, while bases taste bitter/soapy and turn red litmus blue. We use indicators like turmeric an...
Begin with a 'Mystery Taste' activity (using safe, diluted substances) or visual prompts of sour vs. soapy items.
Introduce Antoine Lavoisier's naming convention: Acidus (sour), Base (alkali).
List properties on the board: Acids taste sour and turn blue litmus red; Bases taste bitter/soapy and turn red litmus blue.
Discuss natural sources: Citric acid in citrus, Lactic acid in curd, etc.
